coredata.dc:title: Sea urchin-like cobalt selenide/N-doped carbon dots as efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for water electrolysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, Xiaojuan
Shi, Yanlong
Niu, Weipeng
NanaZhang,
Wang, Cheng
Lei, Yonggang
Yang, Hongyu
Wang, Hongyan
coredata.dc:description: Designing high-efficient, durable and low-cost bifunctional electrocatalysts is of great significance to realize green hydrogen production by electrolytic water splitting. Herein, 3D sea urchin-like CoSe2 arrays modified with nitrogen doped carbon dots grown on Ni foam (CoSe2/NCDs/NF) was successfully constructed via an easy hydrothermal reaction combined with anion exchange process. The designed CoSe2/NCDs/NF catalyst exhibits enhanced catalytic activity and stability for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in different electrolyte, with reduced overpotential of 268 mV to afford 70 mA cm−2 for OER and low overpotentials of 94/102 mV in acid/alkaline solution at 10 mA cm−2 for HER, respectively. Impressively, when assembling a two-electrode alkaline electrolyzer with CoSe2/NCDs/NF simultaneously acts as the anode and cathode, the cell voltage of only 1.61 V is obtained to drive 30 mA cm−2, with robust stability(for at least 70 h). The excellent catalytic activity is attributed that the 3D sea urchin-like structure significantly augments exposure of active sites and facilitates mass/charge transport; the synergy effect of NCDs and CoSe2 effectively promotes electron transfer, optimizes electronic environment, and adjusts the adsorption/desorption free energy of the intermediates; and in situ growth of materials and introduction of carbon materials ensures structural stability and enhances the electrical conductivity. This work provides a feasible guidance for the rational design of transition metal selenide catalysts with outstanding performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped
Carbon quantum dots
Cobalt selenide
Electrocatalysis
Overall water splitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrations of the fabrication procedures of CoSe2@NCDs/NF.
FESEM image of (a, b) CoSe2/NF, (c, d) CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5, (e, f) CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 at different magnifications; (g, h) HRTEM image and elements mappings of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 sample.
(a) XRD pattern, (b) FT-IR spectra of CoSe2, NCDs and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum, and high resolution XPS spectrum for (b) C 1 s, (c) Co 2p, (d) Se 3d of CoSe2 and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5.
(a) Polarization curves; (b) Tafel plots; (c) Nyquist plots; (d) Cdl values of the prepared samples; (e) Multicurrent step diagram of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5; (f) Stability tests of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5.

(a, c, e, g, i) Polarization curves, Tafel plots, Nyquist plots, Cdl values, long-term stability of the prepared samples in the acid solution; (b, d, f, h, j) Polarization curves, Tafel plots, Nyquist plots, Cdl values, long-term stability of the prepared samples in the alkali solution.

(a) LSV curves of Pt/C||RuO2 and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5||CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 in a two-electrode electrolyzer; (b) The choronoamperometric curves of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5||CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 electrolyzer; (c, d) Gas collection device of water splitting;(e) The theoretical calculation and experimental measurement of the gas volume of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5.

head.ce:title: Sea urchin-like cobalt selenide/N-doped carbon dots as efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for water electrolysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Hexi Corridor Resources Utilization of Gansu Universities, HeXi University, Zhangye, Gansu 734000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Hexi Corridor Resources Utilization of Gansu Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Hexi Corridor Resources Utilization of Gansu Universities, HeXi University, Zhangye, Gansu 734000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing high-efficient, durable and low-cost bifunctional electrocatalysts is of great significance to realize green hydrogen production by electrolytic water splitting. Herein, 3D sea urchin-like CoSe2 arrays modified with nitrogen doped carbon dots grown on Ni foam (CoSe2/NCDs/NF) was successfully constructed via an easy hydrothermal reaction combined with anion exchange process. The designed CoSe2/NCDs/NF catalyst exhibits enhanced catalytic activity and stability for oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in different electrolyte, with reduced overpotential of 268 mV to afford 70 mA cm−2 for OER and low overpotentials of 94/102 mV in acid/alkaline solution at 10 mA cm−2 for HER, respectively. Impressively, when assembling a two-electrode alkaline electrolyzer with CoSe2/NCDs/NF simultaneously acts as the anode and cathode, the cell voltage of only 1.61 V is obtained to drive 30 mA cm−2, with robust stability(for at least 70 h). The excellent catalytic activity is attributed that the 3D sea urchin-like structure significantly augments exposure of active sites and facilitates mass/charge transport; the synergy effect of NCDs and CoSe2 effectively promotes electron transfer, optimizes electronic environment, and adjusts the adsorption/desorption free energy of the intermediates; and in situ growth of materials and introduction of carbon materials ensures structural stability and enhances the electrical conductivity. This work provides a feasible guidance for the rational design of transition metal selenide catalysts with outstanding performance.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing energy crisis and environmental deterioration induced by sharp consumption of fossil fuels have promoted the development of renewable energy storage and conversion technologies [1,2]. Hydrogen (H2) is identified as the most ideal alternative for traditional fossil fuels owing to its high gravimetric energy density and zero oxycarbide emission [3,4]. Electrocatalytic water splitting, involving oxygen evolution reaction (OER) on the anode and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) on the cathode, is one of promising alternative for green hydrogen generation [5–10]. However, the high overpotentials and the sluggish kinetics of OER and HER hinder the energy conversion efficiency of water electrolysis technology [11]. Although noble metal Pt- and Ru- based electrocatalysts exhibit excellent performance, the scarcity and high cost dramatically impede their widespread application [12]. Accordingly, it is extremely urgent to seek fast kinetics, high activity, and stable bifunctional catalysts towards HER and OER in different electrolytes.

Currently, transitional metal selenides such as CoSe2 [13,14], NiSe [15], and FeSe2 [16] have attracted much attention due to their modulated electron configuration, flexible structure, abundant chemical states and low cost [17]. Among them, CoSe2 has demonstrated outstanding electrocatalysis on account of the optimal electronic structure of t2g 6eg 1. For example, Du et al. developed a hollow urchin-like CoSe2 with high surface area exhibiting efficient OER activity [18]. However, its low ionic conductivity, poor stability and insufficient active sites restrict the effective water splitting performance. Currently, many optimization strategies have been proposed such as interface engineering [19], composites construction [20–22] and electronic regulation [23], but the electrocatalytic activities of the selenide for water electrolysis are still unsatisfactory, should be further improved.

Carbon materials are diffusely applied in the construction of electrocatalysts on account of excellent electrical conductivity and good corrosion resistance for acid or alkali solutions [24]. Carbon dots (CDs), as 0 D carbon nanomaterials, possess outstanding properties such as rapid charge transfer, abundant defect sites, simple synthesis, high surface area, low price, which are conducive to design the multifunctional CDs-based electrocatalyst [25–27]. Recent studies have demonstrated that the doping heteroatoms (such as B, N, F and P) can obviously improve the electrocatalytic activity of the composites [28]. Among them, the introduction of nitrogen can generate intimate and strong interactions between metal and carbon, which can further enhance the stability and catalytic performance of the corresponding composites [29]. The NCDs with negative charge can attract positive metal particles, and regulate the growth of crystal, finally construct the 3D structure of complexes, which can effectively prevent the agglomeration of the active materials [30]. In addition, NCDs also own rich functional groups, such as-OH, –COOH, which can supply rich active sites for the electrocatalytic reactions [31]. Taking the above comprehensive considerations, it is expected that NCDs coupled cobalt selenide can own a well-design structure with a large number of active centers, faster charge transfer and more abundant transmission channels, so as to further improve the electrocatalytic performance of water decomposition.

In view of the aforementioned factors, CoSe2 combined with NCDs in situ grown on NF substrate (CoSe2/NCDs/NF) was constructed as highly efficient electrocatalyst via simple in situ hydrothermal and selenization processes. As expected, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF presents high catalytic activity for OER with low overpotential of 268 to deliver the current density of 70 mA·cm−2. Besides, CoSe2/NCDs/NF also displays a remarkable HER performance with low potentials of 94, 102 mV in 0.5 M H2SO4 and 1.0 M KOH, respectively. Impressively, CoSe2/NCDs/NF bifunctional electrode acquires a current density of 30 mA cm−2 at a low applied voltage of 1.61 V and stably catalyzes for 70 h at constant potential within only 7.4 % activity attenuation. This work furnishes an insightful guidance for the rational designing of the outstanding non-precious metal–carbon matrix electrocatalysts and especially, accelerates the broad applications of NCDs-based materials in the field of water electrolysis. The catalyst exhibits outstanding catalytic performance benefiting from the following factors: (i) the more exposure of active sites ensures the accessibility of the electrolyte; (ii) the Co-N-C bonding generates by hybridization and tunes electronic structures of the composites; (iii) the 3D urchin-like structure accelerates gas bubble release and the mass/charge transfer.

Negative charge nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) were prepared by an improved hydrothermal method [32]. Typically, citric acid (40 mmol) and ethylenediamine (2.68 mL) were dispersed in ultrapure water (80 mL). After stirring for 30 min, the obtained clear solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 180 ℃ for 10 h to gain dark-brown product. The obtained product was added into water and then was performed to dialysis for 48 h. In order to further purify the carbon quantum dots, the dialyzed samples were evaporated into a viscous liquid by the rotary evaporator. Finally, the NCDs powder was gained after freeze-drying.

Firstly, 6 mmol CoCl2·6H2O, 6 mmol urea and different amount of NCDs powder (5, 10, 25, 50, 100 and 200 mg, corresponding to CoSe2/NCDs/NF-x, x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6) were added into 40 mL deionized water following by 30 min sonication to form a pink solution. Next, the homogeneous solution was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave with the pre-treated Ni foam (NF) and reacted at 100 ℃ for 12 h before cooling to room temperature. The Co(OH)2/NCDs/NF sample was collected and cleaned by deionized water and ethanol, and then dried at 70 ℃ for 5 h.

0.059 g selenium powder was dissolve in 1.5 mL deionized water and 0.065 g sodium borohydride was dispersed in 30 mL absolute ethanol. Then, the two solutions were mixed and added into autoclave with the Co(OH)2/NCDs/NF precursor. After heating at 140 ℃ for 12 h, the sample was washed with deionized water and ethanol, respectively. Finally, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF catalyst was achieved after drying at 70 ℃ for 8 h.
The preparation process of the CoSe2/NF is similar to the procedure of CoSe2/NCDs/NF, without the adding of NCDs.

The electrochemical tests were performed in different electrolytes by CHI660E electrochemical workstation integrated into standard three electrode system, in which as-obtained catalysts with an approximate loading of 5.40 mg cm−2 as working electrode, graphite rod as counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as reference electrode. All electrochemical data were repeated more than 3 times to ensure the accuracy. The acquired potentials were calibrated versus RHE via the equation: ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 + 0.059 pH [33]. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was acquired at the potential range 0 V ∼ -0.4 V vs RHE for HER and 1.0 ∼ 1.6 V vs RHE for OER and a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1.

The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were noted at open-circuit potential to support in a frequency range of 105 ∼ 10-1 Hz. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) plots were measured at the different scan rates of 10–100 mV s−1 in the potential range of 0.25–0.35 V (νs. RHE). The long-term stability of the as-fabricated samples was performed at a constant voltage of 1.42 V vs RHE for OER and at −0.105 V for HER by the chronoamperometry (CA) test. The catalytic activity for overall water splitting (OWS) was assessed in two-electrode electrolyzer, and the as-fabricated catalysts as both cathode and anode in alkaline media. To evaluate the performance of OWS, the chronoamperometric test was recorded at the potential corresponding to support a current density of about 10 mA·cm−2 for 70 h and LSV curve was collected from 0.5 to 1.8 V.

The Tafel slope was plotted based on the LSV plots using the equation:
η = a + b ln j
where a is a constant, η represents the overpotential, j is the current density, and b is the Tafel slope.

The drainage method was utilized to assess the Faradic efficiency (FE), in which the volume ratios of produced gas were easily calculated. The calculation formula of FE was as follows:
FE =
nF
n
gas
jt

where n is the transferred electron number, F is the Faraday constant, ngas is the amount of generated gas at the cathode and the anode, and t is the time for the constant oxidation/reduction current.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were detected to insight the crystallographic structure by PANalytical X'Pert3 diffractometre. The morphological structure was carried out on Quanta 450 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and F200X transmission electron microscope (TEM). The chemical compositions and valence states were characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, PHI-5072). The surface functional groups of the catalyst were analyzed via Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, iS50).

The synthetic route of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF catalyst is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 . Initially, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCDs) were prepared by an improved bottom-up procedure. Subsequently, NCDs modifying Co(OH)2 were grown on the surface of the NF substrate via a simple hydrothermal treatment. Finally, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF were fabricated by selenizing the Co(OH)2/NCDs/NF precursors via a low-temperature selenization approach with Se powder as selenium source. Additionally, the experiments also explore that the different content of the NCDs regulate the morphologies of the catalysts. It can be obviously seen that CoSe2/NCDs/NF with proper NCDs presents a 3D urchin-like structure which favor for the catalysis reaction for water splitting.

To further analyze the structural characteristics of the samples, the as-obtained catalysts were characterized by FESEM as shown in Fig. 2 (a-f). As depicted in Fig. 2 (a, b), the morphology of CoSe2 without NCDs presents linear nanoneedles structure with smooth surface. When 100 mg NCDs is introduced into the CoSe2/NF (Fig. 2c, d), the nanoneedles are attached with carbon quantum dots, and the distance between the nanoneedles is reduced, which forms a 3D sea urchin-like structure with a size of about 10 μm. The nanoneedles show a radial distribution. The open, uniform structure increases the number of active sites, and provides more contact area between active species and electrolyte, which is crucial for improving electrochemical performance. When the amount of NCDs is increased to 200 mg (Fig. 2e, f), the gaps between the nanoneedles are gradually filled with NCDs, which makes the nanoneedles denser and changes the urchin-like morphology into microspheres, thus reducing the exposure of active sites. From the above analysis, it can be seen that NCDs can effectively regulate the morphology of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF composites. TEM and HRTEM were conducted to further gain insight into the compositions and structures. Fig. S1 demonstrates the unique morphology of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5. The HRTEM image(Fig. 2g) presents that the lattice fringes with the inter-planar spacing of 0.262 nm is related to the (210) plane of CoSe2 crystal and the 0.21 nm lattice spacing match with the (100) plane of NCDs, respectively[34]. Elemental mappings (Fig. 2h) clearly display that the Co, Se, N, C, and O elements are homogeneous distributed in the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 sample, further indicating the electrocatalyst is successfully fabricated.

The phase composition and crystal structure of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 were analyzed by XRD patterns in Fig. 3 a. The broad peaks at 7° and 23° are assigned to highly disordered carbon phase in NCDs materials [34]. The diffraction peaks of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 locates at 8, 16.1, 26.1, 33.2, 36.1, 38.7, 45.1, 52.8, 55.3 and 62.6° corresponding to (006), (101), (011), (111), (200), (210), (121), (031), (131) and (122) planes of CoSe2 crystal (PDF#53–0449)[35], respectively, indicating the formation of the CoSe2 phase in CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5. The intensity of diffraction peaks of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 composites obviously decreases compared to CoSe2, indicating that the strong interactions between CoSe2 and NCDs. This is an important evidence for the formation of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 composite.

The FTIR spectra of the CoSe2, NCDs and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 samples were conducted as shown in Fig. 3b. The absorption peak of CoSe2 at 3509 cm−1 is accredited to the stretching vibration of Co-Se. In NCDs, the peak at 1660 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibration absorption of C = O, the peak at 1540 cm−1 is ascribed to the bending vibration of N–H, and the asymmetric stretching vibration band near 1300 cm−1 is assigned to the presence of C-NH-C[36]. For CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5, it can be observed that its peaks are similar to CoSe2, and the peaks at 830 cm−1 and 932 cm−1 are accredited to the vibration of CO3 2–, verifying the presence of inserted CO3 2– [37]. In addition, the intensity of C-NH-C in CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 is obviously decrease compared to CoSe2, further displaying the strong interaction between CoSe2 and NCDs. The results indicate that CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 composites are successful constructed.

The surface chemical composition and electronic structure of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 was evaluated by XPS spectra. The survey spectrum of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 in Fig. 4 a verifies the existence of Co, O, N, C and Se elements. Additional, the O elements in the spectra may be come from the oxidation of the catalyst surface in the air [38]. The peaks of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 locate at 178.7, 497.1 and 979.7 eV, which correspond to Se and O auger peaks. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4b) of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 includes four major peaks, corresponding to C–C, C-N, C-O and C = O [39]. In the Co 2p region (Fig. 4c), the peaks at the binding energies of 781.1, 784.2, 796.9 and 802.9 eV are indexed to Co-N, Co-Se, Co-O, and satellite peaks, respectively [40]. The Se 3d spectrum (Fig. 4d) contains four peaks with binding energies of 54.11 eV and 55.62 eV corresponding to Se 3d 5/2 and Se 3d 3/2, and 58.99 and 62.31 eV satellite peaks are caused by the surface oxidation in the air to produce SeOx species [41]. Compared with bare CoSe2, the negative shift of Co and Se was presented, signifying the presence of strong interfacial interaction between CoSe2 nanowires and NCDs, which can favorable to modulate the electronic structure of the catalysts and generate synergistic effects to improve catalytic performance.

The electrocatalytic reaction kinetics behavior is assessed by Tafel slopes of the corresponding catalyst, which are derived from linear fitting of LSV curves. As depicted in Fig. 5b, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 possesses a Tafel slope of 172 mV dec-1, which is smaller than those of CoSe2/NF (255 mV dec-1), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-1 (237 mV dec-1), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-2 (227 mV dec-1), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-3 (209 mV dec-1), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-4 (194 mV dec-1), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-6 (195 mVdec-1), verifying the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 can expose abundant accessible active sites owing to abundant hierarchical interfaces and high affinity of the sample. This process is consistent with steps (1) and (2) of the reaction pathway, which is an essential elementary step of OH coordination to the active site. The simplified OER mechanism of the catalyst in alkaline media, which is given below [43]:
(1)
M + O
H -
→ M - O
H ads
+
e -
(2)
M - O
H ads
+ O
H -
→ M -
O
ads
+
H 2
O +
e -
(3)
M -
O
ads
+ O
H -
→ M - O O
H ads
+
e -
(4)
M - O O
H ads
+ O
H -
→ M +
O 2
+
H 2
O +
e -

where M is the active site of the material surface, and “ads” is the adsorbed species of the electrocatalyst surface. Based on the Tafel value of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5, the rate limiting step is accredited to Eq.4, implying the formation of O = O bond [44].

The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is appointed to reveal the intrinsic charge transport characteristic. The Rct represents charge transfer resistance from the electrolyte and the catalysts, which plays an important role for evaluating the reaction kinetics and can be obtained from the diameters of these semicircles. As displayed in Fig. 5d, compared to the Rct values of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-1 (63.6 Ω), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-2（40.2 Ω), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-3 (25.6 Ω), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-4 (22.4 Ω), and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-6 (19.3 Ω), the smaller Rct value of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 (8.9 Ω) verifies that the addition of NCDs further synergistically promotes charge transfer kinetics and enhances the intrinsic catalytic activity for OER reaction. Furthermore, the electrochemical double layer capacitance (Cdl) is also measured to evaluate the intrinsic activities by CV at different sweep speeds (10–100 mV s−1). According to the relationship: ESCA = Cdl/CS, the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) is proportional to the Cdl. As shown in Fig. 5e, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 exhibits the highest Cdl value (58.1 mF cm−2), which is superior to CoSe2/NF (5.6 mF cm−2), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-1 (29.3 mF cm−2), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-2 (39.9 mF cm−2), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-3 (41.5 mF cm−2), CoSe2/NCDs/NF-4 (52.5 mF cm−2) and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-6 (53.8 mF cm−2), suggesting the electrode exposes more active surface area. This is attributed to the unique structure of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 with homogeneous distribution of active substance on the 3D sea urchin interfaces, which offer numerous exposed active sites and open internal space for the electrochemical reactions.

In addition, electrochemical stability is another vital index to evaluate the practical application. Fig. 5e shows the multi-step chronopotentiometric curve of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5. At 10 mA cm−2, the corresponding potential immediately responds to the current density and remains stable for 500 s. The potential change is very negligible in every current density, indicating that CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 catalyst presents excellent mass transfer, good electrical conductivity and mechanical robustness. Besides, a long-term stability was also reported in Fig. 5f. The current density only decreases by 1.8 mA cm−2 after long-term cycling for 70 h, suggesting the excellent stability of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 for OER process. The good stability should be attributed to the compact contact between conductive substrate and active substance, and the hierarchical self-supporting structure of the catalysts. After OER, SEM results (Fig. S1a) reveal that the skeletons of the catalyst can be well remained, verifying it owns good stability. Further XPS spectra were utilized to determine the chemical state of the catalyst after OER (Fig. S1b, c). Compared with the previous OER, it is clear that the intensity of the Co 2p and Se 3d peaks are significantly reduced, indicating that the surface of the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 is oxidized, which is consistent with previous reports [45].The peaks of Co and Se slightly shift after the OER stability test, proving that some irreversible reaction still happens on the surface of the electrode [31].

Surprisingly, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 not only has excellent OER performance in alkaline solutions, but also owns high HER activity in the different electrolytes. LSV curves of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 and other comparison samples were displayed in Fig. 6 a, b. CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 manifests an obvious reduced overpotential compared with other counterpart, only 94 mV to arrive 10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. Similarly, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 also presents a lower overpotential 102 mV in 1 M KOH. Even better, the HER catalytic activity of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 outperforms many previously reported electrocatalysts based on non-precious metals (Table S1), which can be attributed to that the strong synergistic effects can expose abundant active sites, and the interfacial interactions between NCDs and CoSe2 are conducive to enhance the intrinsic activity by optimizing the adsorption/desorption energy of intermediates in the catalytic reaction. As displayed in Fig. 6c and d, the measured Tafel slope is 73/76 mV dec-1 in acid/alkaline solution for CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5, which is in accordance with the phenomenon that the current density remarkable enhances with the increase of potential. The Tafel slope value of 76 mV dec-1 strongly implies that it undergoes the Volmer-Heyrovsky pathway, and the Heyrovsky step is the HER rate-determine step [46]:
(5)
H 2
O +
e
-
→
H
ads
+ O
H -
(6)
H 2
O +
e
-
+
H
ads
→
H 2
+ O
H -

Furthermore, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 also exhibits the lowest charge transfer resistance in the Nyquist Plot (Fig. 6 (e, f)), signifying that fast charge transfer capacity and enhanced conductivity in the different electrolytes. ECSA is measured to further evaluate the kinetics mechanism of HER reaction. Notably, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 catalyst has the maximal Cdl value of 32.4/46.1 mF cm−2 in acid/alkaline solution in comparison to CoSe2/NF, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-1, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-2, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-3, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-4 and CoSe2/NCDs/NF-6, confirming that CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 possesses the best intrinsic activity. The long-time durability of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 was recorded by chronopotentiometry test for 70 h at a constant potential (Fig. 6 (i, j)). It is noteworthy that the current density exhibits negligible change of about 2.7 % and 12.4 % in acid/alkaline solution. Furthermore, CoSe2/NCDs-5 was conducted under multi-current steps of 10 ∼ 110 mA cm−2. With the stepwise change of the current density, the potential keeps basically steady at each stage (Fig. S2 a,b). The stable and fast potential response proves that the catalysts own superior stability and rapid mass transfer and good conductivity. According to the above results and analysis, CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 is regarded as the material with excellent stability and rapid potential response ability. The CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 sample was also characterized after long-term HER test to evaluate morphological and chemical composition changes. The SEM images (Fig. S2 c, d) display that slight morphological transformation compared to the pristine CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5, but the original sea urchin-like structure of the catalyst is still inherited. XPS spectra (Fig. S2 e,f) exhibit the comparison of Co 2p and Se 3d of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 before and after cycling. For the Co 2p spectrum, the Co 2p 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2 peaks decrease and shift towards the positive binding energy in contrast to before long-term testing, revealing the formation of Co-O bonds and the appearance of Co3+ in CoOOH [47]. In the Se 3d region, the peak (Se 3d 5/2 and Se 3d 3/2) position also shows positive displacement, and the peak intensity of SeOx exhibits an obviously increase, implying the surface oxidation of the catalyst.

Encouraged by the excellent HER and OER properties, an alkaline electrolyzer is constructed equipped with CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 as both cathode and anode for OWS. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5||CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 illustrates the superior catalytic activity that only requires a cell voltage of 1.61 V to arrive the current density of 30 mA cm−2 for water electrolysis, which is superior to the Pt/C||RuO2 commercial catalyst as well as other Co-based bifunctional catalysts reported (Table S3). The remarkable OWS performance of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 can also be verified by plenty of gas bubbles generated from the surface of the two electrodes. Even more impressively, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 also exhibits long-term stability for 70 h at a constant potential, the current density remains 92.6 % of the initial activities after long-term test, revealing the excellent stability of CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 (Fig. 7 b). We also collect the produced hydrogen and oxygen by drainage method (Fig. 7c, d), the volume ratio of H2 and O2 is about 2:1, which is consistent with the theoretical value, and the Faradaic efficiency (FE) values are close to 100 % (Fig. 7e), the energy loss is about 0. It indicates that the catalyst with outstanding activity and stability can be applied in long-time practical electrolysis of OWS.

In conclusion, a stable and high-efficiency CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 was designed by combining facile hydrothermal treatment with selenization process. The synergistic activation of CoSe2 and NCDs increases active sites and optimizes electronic structure for improving catalytic reaction kinetics. Therefore, the CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 displays excellent bifunctional catalytic activity with low overpotentials of 102 and 268 mV for HER and OER at 10 and 70 mA cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH along with 94 mV for HER at 10 mA cm−2 in 0.5 M H2SO4. Surprisingly, the alkaline electrolyzer based on CoSe2/NCDs/NF-5 only requires 1.61 V operating voltage to deliver 30 mA cm−2, which is compared with the activity of a commercial Pt/C||RuO2, and along with strong stability for at least 70 h. This work provides a useful guideline to design high activity bifunctional electrocatalysts for broadening industrial prospects in energy conversion and storage domains.
